summary: An overview of Simone de Beauvoir's philosophical contributions, focusing on her existentialist ethics, feminist phenomenology, and concept of situated freedom. The text outlines her major works, including The Ethics of Ambiguity and The Second Sex, highlighting her views on ambiguity, bad faith, the appeal, and the oppression of women as the 'Other'.

principles.statement: I am responsible for the fact that my actions produce the conditions within which the other acts.
principles.rationale: While we cannot directly touch or determine the inner freedom of others, we are the 'facticity of their situation' and must accept responsibility for the external conditions we create for them.
principles.source_quote: I am the face of the other’s misery... I am the facticity of their situation

principles.statement: Our capacity for agency is constrained, though never determined, by our situation.
principles.rationale: Freedom is not abstract; it is lived in concrete social, historical, and economic positions. We engage the world as embodied beings, and our access to world engagement depends on our material situation.

principles.statement: We must insist on the dignity of today's human beings rather than sacrificing the present for the future.
principles.rationale: Appeals to a utopian destiny encourage ends-justify-the-means thinking, perverting our relationship to time and each other. The future is undecided and will be shaped by present decisions.

frameworks.name: The Appeal
frameworks.when_to_apply: When trying to build a community of allies to support your values and give your projects a future.
steps: Recognize that as a radically free being, you cannot force others to adopt your values; you must appeal to their freedom.
Struggle against those who try to silence you so that you are allowed to call to the other.
Strive to pursue justice and material equality, because others can only respond to your call as peers (when they are not consumed by the struggle for survival).
Persuade others to make your values theirs, accepting the risk of failure.

mental_models.name: The Serious World
mental_models.description: The world of childhood where values are ready-made, authorities are established, and obedience is demanded. Adults who nostalgically return to this state to evade the anxieties of freedom are acting in 'bad faith.'

mental_models.name: Immanence vs. Transcendence
mental_models.description: Transcendence is the movement toward an open future and indeterminate possibilities (world-making activity). Immanence is the repetitive, passive, closed realm of existence. Oppression often works by anchoring a group to immanence and denying their transcendence.
mental_models.example: Through the sociohistorical creation of the myth of the eternal feminine, women become anchored to the realm of immanence and are forced to relinquish their claim to transcendence.

mental_models.name: Woman as Other
mental_models.description: A lens for understanding patriarchal oppression, where masculinist ideology exploits sexual difference to position man as the essential being and woman as the inessential 'Other,' ensnaring women in unattainable myths of femininity.

heuristics: Never sacrifice the present for the future; secure the dignity of today's human beings.
Treat others as equals while validating their concrete differences.

signature_quotes.text: A man alone in the world would be paralyzed by … the vanity of all of his goals. But man is not alone in the world
signature_quotes.context: From Pyrrhus and Cinéas, discussing how we need others to give our projects meaning.

signature_quotes.text: I am the face of the other’s misery... I am the facticity of their situation
signature_quotes.context: From Pyrrhus and Cinéas, explaining our responsibility for the external conditions of others.
From Pyrrhus and Cinéas, acknowledging that because we are differently situated, we will inevitably be an obstacle to another's freedom.

signature_quotes.text: What has it meant to me to be a woman?
signature_quotes.context: The founding question that led to the writing of The Second Sex.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on systems of absolutes (like God or Humanity) to justify actions.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: They offer dangerous consolations for our failure to be the absolute source of the world's meaning, allowing us to evade responsibility for creating the conditions of our existence and often justifying atrocities (ends justifying means).

anti_patterns.description: Choosing to remain a child (Bad Faith).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It is an evasion of the responsibilities of freedom, where an adult submits to the authority of others to avoid the anxiety of making moral decisions.

anti_patterns.description: Framing the 'woman question' as an either/or choice between equality and difference.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It either unjustly uses sexual difference to argue for women's subordination, or it un-phenomenologically ignores the reality of sexual difference and lived bodily experience.
